key_insight: MCL-PINNs combines phase-field physics with a discrete-time, multi-network PINN design to accurately track moving contact lines and sharp interfacial evolution under dynamic boundary conditions.
engineering_relevance: The method offers a mesh-free solver for difficult wetting and two-phase flow simulations, which is useful for designing and analyzing microfluidic, coating, and interfacial transport systems.
method: Discrete-time time-marching with separate networks per step
Relaxed output distribution and variable scaling for sharp phase-field features
Adaptive loss weighting and collocation sampling with interface extraction
evidence_signal: The approach was validated on three benchmark problems—droplet coalescence, shear-induced deformation, and heterogeneous-channel wetting—and reported to outperform standard PINNs in accuracy and robustness.
watch_item: Training can still be sensitive to adaptive sampling, loss balancing, and scaling choices, especially for long-time simulations with highly localized contact-line dynamics.
paper_title: A phase-field neural solver for moving contact line problems with dynamic boundary conditions
